title: Chapter 2: Give Me Your Voice
content: Dawn bled through the window like a slow bruise.

Aline hadn't slept. She'd tried — once, maybe twice — but every time she closed her eyes, the melody was there, threading through the dark spaces behind her eyelids like a wire she couldn't cut. She'd been sitting on the edge of her bed since four in the morning, phone cradled in both hands like something fragile and dangerous, thumb hovering over the message thread.
The text was gone. She'd checked twelve times.

The first six, she'd scrolled up and down with her heart hammering, certain she'd missed it, certain it was still there buried under old threads and forgotten contacts. By the eighth check, she was calm — the performative kind, the kind you put on for a bathroom mirror. By the twelfth, she'd stopped pretending and just stared at the empty space where the message had been, the silence of it louder than the voice had been.
But the melody remained.

She let her head fall back against the wall and replayed it — not from the phone, from memory. The shower melody. The half-formed thing she'd hummed to herself with water running hot over her shoulders, a fragment of song that had lived in her throat since she was seventeen. She'd sung it without thinking. A private moment. A nothing.
And he'd sent it back.

Not just echoed. Not just remembered and repeated. He'd produced it. There were layers now — a bass line that pulsed like a second heartbeat, reverb that wrapped around her melody like cupped hands, a beat that shouldn't have worked with her tempo but somehow did. It was the kind of production that took a real studio hours. Days, maybe. The kind of production that turned a shower hum into something you'd hear in a sold-out venue.
Not good.
Perfect.
Whoever this was — whatever this was — the person behind that voice wasn't just a hacker with access to her phone. They were a genius.
Aline set the phone down on the mattress and pressed her palms against her eyes until colors bloomed. She tried the rational angles, lined them up like medical instruments on a tray.

Hacked phone. Possible. Probable, even. She'd clicked links she shouldn't have, used public Wi-Fi at coffee shops that smelled like burnt espresso and broken ambitions, downloaded apps she'd never bothered to research. Her digital hygiene was a joke. Anyone with moderate skill and enough patience could have gotten in.

Prank. Less possible. Pranks didn't produce music like that. Pranks didn't hold the kind of stillness that voice held — that measured, unhurried cadence that made every word feel like it had been chosen years ago and simply waited for the right moment to arrive.

Stalker. The word sat heavier than the others. She should be scared. She knew this the way she knew the sky was blue — factually, distantly, as something that didn't require her immediate attention but probably should. A stranger had listened to her in the shower. Had recorded her. Had sent something back. The violation was so thorough it circled past horror into something numb and quiet, like pressing a bruise until the pain becomes just another sensation.

She pushed herself off the bed. The floor was cold under her bare feet, and the shock of it moved up through her ankles like a warning she chose not to parse. She walked to the bathroom, flicked the light on, and faced herself.

The mirror showed her what she already knew. Dark circles under her eyes that no amount of concealer would fully hide — she'd stopped buying the expensive kind four months ago. Chapped lips, the lower one split in the corner where she'd bitten through the skin sometime in the night without noticing. Hair tangled and dull, pressed flat on one side where she'd leaned against the wall for too long. The face of a woman who'd been told "no" so many times she'd forgotten the shape of "yes."

She gripped the edge of the sink. The porcelain was cold and slightly damp, and she could smell the faint mineral tang of the hard water that left white residue on everything it touched. She looked past her own reflection to the kitchen visible through the open door.

The eviction notice sat on the counter where she'd left it three days ago, the red stamp at the bottom like a small wound. She'd stopped putting it in a drawer. There was no point hiding something that had already burrowed into every thought she had. Three days was what they'd given her. Three days was yesterday. Two days now, and the countdown wasn't a number — it was a sensation, a tightening in her chest every time she thought about it, which was constantly.
She released the sink and walked back to the bedroom. The phone was still there, screen dark, patient. She was reaching for it when it buzzed.
Unknown number.
She should let it go to voicemail. She should do a lot of things.
Her thumb pressed accept.
Silence. The long, held-breath kind. Then—
"Good morning, little bird."

The same voice. Low and unhurried, with that quality of controlled warmth she'd heard last night. But this time it wasn't filtered through a text-to-speech artifact or buried under static. It was clear. Clean. And close — so close it felt like he was standing directly behind her, lips near her ear, breath brushing the fine hairs at the nape of her neck.
She spun.
Empty room. The bed, the phone, the morning light pooling on the floorboards like spilled milk. Nothing else.
"How—"

"How doesn't matter." He cut her off gently, the way you'd redirect a child reaching for something sharp. "What matters is this: tonight you'll record one song. Just one. I'll send you everything you need. You sing, I'll do the rest. By tomorrow morning, no one in this city will be able to look away from you."

Her hand was shaking. She could feel the phone trembling against her ear, a tiny seismic event she couldn't control. She should hang up. Should call the police. Should call someone — anyone. The rational angles were still lined up on the tray, gleaming and sterile, and she could see them clearly.
She didn't reach for any of them.
"What do you want from me?"

The question came out raw, stripped of the composure she'd been clinging to. There was a pause — long enough that she checked the screen to make sure the call hadn't dropped. The silence had weight. It pressed against her ear like a hand.
"Your voice." Each word distinct. Deliberate. "Your trust. Your loyalty. That's all."
"And if I say no?"
Another pause. When he spoke again, his tone hadn't shifted — still patient, still warm — but something underneath it changed, the way the temperature of a room drops just before a storm.
"Then I'll still hear you sing at The Hollow on Thursday. You'll still be extraordinary. And in three weeks, you'll be sleeping in your car."

Not a threat. That was the worst part. He wasn't threatening her. There was no menace in his voice, no edge, no sharpened point designed to intimidate. He was stating a fact. The rent. The eviction. The savings account she'd drained in November when the alternator died and the dental bill arrived in the same week. The math was simple, and he'd done it, and the truth of it landed in her chest like a stone dropped into still water.

Three weeks. She'd calculated the same thing herself. Three weeks until her savings ran out completely and the gap between her income and her survival became a chasm she couldn't bridge. He'd seen the numbers. Of course he had. He'd seen her sing in the shower. Why wouldn't he see the rest?
She ended the call without saying goodbye.
The courier arrived in fifty-three minutes.

Aline knew because she sat by the window and counted the seconds — not deliberately, not as a choice, but because time had become granular, each minute a grain of sand falling through a narrow glass, and she couldn't stop watching it. The knock at the door made her flinch so hard her knee hit the underside of the coffee table.

The courier was a young man with a shaved head and earbuds dangling from his collar. He held a matte black case — rectangular, heavy-looking, unmarked — and a clipboard. She signed without reading the form. He was gone before she could ask a question, and she wouldn't have asked one anyway because her throat had closed around something that felt like terror and hope compressed into the same dense, impossible knot.
She carried the case to the kitchen table and opened it.

The foam interior was precisely cut, each component nestled in its own compartment like surgical instruments in a case. A condenser microphone — she recognized the brand, had drooled over it in catalogues she couldn't afford. An audio interface with more inputs than she'd ever need. Cables. A pop filter. And a pair of studio headphones that probably cost more than three months of her rent combined.

She lifted the headphones out and turned them over in her hands. The leather ear cushions were soft and cold, and when she brought them close to her face, she could smell the rich, familiar scent — new leather mixed with the faint chemical bite of factory packaging and something underneath that was just the promise of what they could deliver: clarity. Precision. The kind of sound that made you hear yourself the way you actually were, not the way you imagined.

She set them down and picked up the microphone. Cold metal in her palm, heavier than she expected, dense with purpose. The weight of it anchored something in her that had been drifting. She ran her thumb over the grille and felt the fine mesh flex slightly under the pressure.
No return address. No note. No instruction manual. Just equipment — the best she'd ever touched — laid out on her kitchen table like an offering.
She set it up with shaking hands.

The interface connected to her laptop with a single cable. The microphone slotted into the stand included in the case. The headphones plugged in, and the moment she pulled them over her ears, the world shrank to the sealed, intimate space inside the leather cushions — and his voice was there, waiting.
"Whenever you're ready." Calm. Certain. "I'm always listening."

The words moved through the headphones and down her spine like cold water. He wasn't on a call. She hadn't opened an app. The voice simply existed in the headphones, in her ears, in the space between her thoughts, as if it had been encoded into the equipment itself.
She pulled the headphones off and set them on the table. Her hands were still shaking. She could smell the leather lingering on her fingers, mixed with the faint salt of her own palms.
She looked at the microphone.
And she thought about her mother.

Eleanor Rivers. One moment of light — a recording contract she'd signed at twenty-two, a single that had charted for exactly one week in the spring of 1994, a voice that producers said could stop traffic. And then thirty years of darkness. The contract that took ownership of everything she wrote. The label that shelved her second album. The drinking that started as celebration and ended as architecture. The woman who stood in kitchen doorways with rheumy eyes and told Aline not to bother, not to try, not to make the same mistakes, and then forgot she'd said it and said it again.

One moment of light. That was all her mother had gotten. And she'd spent the rest of her life in the shadow it cast, shrinking smaller and smaller until she fit inside the confines of a one-bedroom apartment that smelled like gin and regret, a woman whose voice had been taken from her by people who promised everything and delivered a cage.
Aline looked at the microphone.
The eviction notice was still on the counter. Two days. Her savings were a countdown. Her mother's voice was dust.
She picked up the mic.

The metal was cold against her palm, and she let the cold sink in — let it ground her the way the floor had grounded her when she got out of bed, the way the porcelain sink had grounded her when she'd gripped it and faced the ruin of her face in the mirror. She pulled the headphones back on. The leather sealed around her ears and the world went quiet — that particular studio-quality quiet that wasn't silence but the absence of everything except what you chose to put inside it.
She closed her eyes.
And she sang the song she'd been carrying inside her for eleven years.

It had come to her when she was seventeen, sitting on the fire escape of her mother's apartment, watching the city lights blur through tears she refused to let fall. She'd never written it down. Never recorded it. Never played it for anyone. It wasn't that she'd hidden it — she'd just never found anyone who wanted to hear it. Every open mic, every audition, every desperate email to every A&R contact she'd ever scraped together — they'd all wanted something else. Something marketable. Something that fit the shape of the industry's appetite, which changed every season and never seemed to include a woman with a voice too big for the rooms she was allowed to sing in.
So the song had stayed. Inside her throat, inside her ribs, inside the locked room she kept behind her sternum where all the things she couldn't say lived together in the dark.
She sang it now.

The melody rose from somewhere deeper than her diaphragm — from the soles of her feet, from the base of her spine, from the part of her that had watched her mother dissolve and sworn it would be different. The words weren't about anything specific. They were about everything. About wanting something so badly your bones ached with it and knowing the cost might be everything you are and choosing to want it anyway. About light that burns. About wings that are also cages. About the moment you stop asking permission and just fly.

Her voice filled the headphones and spilled past them. The apartment walls were thin, and she didn't care. She could feel the song moving through her like weather — a warm front pushing against a cold one, the pressure building and releasing, the precipitation of everything she'd held back for eleven years finally, finally falling.
She sang until there was nothing left.
The apartment was silent except for her breathing.

She sat at the kitchen table, the microphone still in her hand, the headphones around her neck. Her chest rose and fell in long, slow waves, and she could taste copper on her tongue — she'd bitten the inside of her cheek at some point and hadn't noticed. The air was warm and close and smelled like leather and her own exertion, the faint animal musk of a body that had given everything it had.

The screen of her laptop glowed — the recording software she hadn't opened was running, a waveform filling the display in green peaks and valleys. She hadn't touched a button. The software had started itself, recorded itself, and now sat waiting like a patient servant.
Her phone screen lit up.
A single word, from no number, no contact, no thread:
**Perfect.**

She waited. Stared at the screen. Waited for more — a demand, a condition, a next step, something that would name the price she'd just agreed to pay. Nothing came. The screen dimmed. The glow faded to black, and she was left with her own reflection ghosted across the glass, pale and indistinct.

She set the phone down and exhaled. Her fingers were trembling again, or still — she'd lost track of when they'd started. She reached for the headphones to take them off, and as her eyes drifted toward the window, toward the morning light now fully established over the rooftops, she noticed something.
The red LED on her laptop camera.
On.

A tiny, steady dot. Crimson and bright and absolutely unambiguous, the way it was when an application was using the camera — recording, streaming, watching. She hadn't opened any application. She hadn't authorized anything. The software that recorded her voice had started itself, and the camera had started itself, and she hadn't noticed because she'd been too busy pouring eleven years of silence into a microphone that a stranger had sent to her door.

Her skin prickled. The sensation started at the base of her neck and cascaded down her arms, raising every hair, turning the air against her body cold and accusatory. She could feel the backs of her eyes burning, and she didn't know if it was tears or just the raw aftermath of the song.
The dot blinked twice.
Once. Twice.
Then went dark.

She stared at the small black lens where the light had been. Her reflection looked back at her — tiny, warped, barely visible in the curved glass. She should feel something specific, she thought. Fear. Violation. Anger. But what moved through her was murkier than any of those, a sediment-clouded current that was part dread and part something she couldn't name and didn't want to examine.
A glitch, she told herself. A software artifact. The laptop was old. Things turned on and off without reason all the time.
She didn't believe it.

She could still smell the leather of the headphones. Still feel the cold dent of the microphone grip in her palm. Still taste copper on her tongue from the cheek she'd bitten through. And somewhere, in the space behind all of that, she could hear his voice — patient, precise, certain — saying the words that had started everything and ended nothing.
*I'm always listening.*

She sat in the silence of her apartment and wondered what else he'd heard. What else he'd seen. And whether the song she'd just given him — the one she'd protected for eleven years — had been a gift or a confession.
The red dot stayed dark.
The morning light kept coming.
